name: Holi
shortDescription: Holi is a vibrant Hindu spring festival celebrating the triumph of good over evil, marked by bonfires and the playful throwing of colored powders.
description: Holi, the 'Festival of Colors,' is a significant Hindu spring festival celebrated predominantly in India and Nepal, but also worldwide amongst the diaspora. It marks the arrival of spring, the end of winter, the blossoming of love, and for many, a festive day to meet others, play and laugh, forget and forgive, and repair broken relationships. The festival is linked to several Hindu myths, most notably the story of Prahlada and Holika, where Prahlada's unwavering devotion to Vishnu allows him to survive Holika's attempt to burn him alive. The burning of Holika symbolizes the victory of good over evil. Another prominent legend revolves around Krishna, who, as a child, complained to his mother Yashoda about his dark complexion compared to Radha's fair skin. Yashoda playfully suggested that Krishna color Radha's face so that they would look similar. This is believed to have inspired the playful throwing of colors during Holi.
subtitle: A riot of colors, a triumph of good.
purpose: To celebrate the triumph of good over evil, the arrival of spring, and to foster social harmony.
participants: Hindus of all ages and backgrounds
timing: The last full moon (Purnima) of the Hindu lunisolar month Phalguna (usually in March).

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: The origins of Holi are deeply rooted in Hindu mythology. The most prominent myth associated with Holi is the story of Prahlada and Holika. Hiranyakashipu, an evil king, demanded that everyone worship him. His son, Prahlada, however, remained devoted to Vishnu. Angered by his son's defiance, Hiranyakashipu plotted with his sister Holika, who possessed a cloak that made her immune to fire, to burn Prahlada alive. Holika sat on a pyre with Prahlada in her lap, but the cloak flew from Holika to Prahlada, saving him while Holika was burned to death. This event is commemorated by the Holika Dahan, the burning of a bonfire on the eve of Holi. Another key myth involves Krishna and Radha, explaining the playful throwing of colors. These stories highlight themes of devotion, good versus evil, and the power of divine intervention.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Holi plays a vital role in Hindu society. It is a time for renewal, forgiveness, and strengthening social bonds. The throwing of colors symbolizes the joy and freedom associated with the arrival of spring, as well as the dissolving of social barriers. The vibrant colors represent energy, life, and happiness. The festival also serves as a reminder of the triumph of good over evil, encouraging individuals to overcome negativity and embrace positivity. Holi provides an opportunity for people from all walks of life to come together, celebrate, and express their joy and unity.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: The symbolism of Holi is multifaceted. The bonfire represents the burning away of negativity, ego, and evil desires. The colored powders symbolize joy, love, and friendship. Each color has a specific meaning: red signifies love and fertility, blue represents Krishna, yellow symbolizes piety, and green stands for new beginnings. The act of throwing colors on each other signifies the breaking down of social barriers and the creation of a sense of community and equality. The festival encourages people to let go of inhibitions, embrace their inner child, and celebrate life to the fullest.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Holi's influence extends beyond its religious significance. It has become a popular cultural event celebrated by people of diverse backgrounds across the globe. Holi celebrations are now common in Western countries, often organized by Hindu communities and cultural organizations. The festival has also been embraced by popular culture, inspiring art, music, and fashion. The vibrant colors and joyful atmosphere of Holi have become synonymous with spring and celebration. The festival's message of unity, forgiveness, and the triumph of good over evil continues to resonate with people worldwide, making it a truly global phenomenon.

sources.author: J.M. Macfie
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Indian myths and legends, including detailed accounts of the Holi festival and its associated stories.

sources.author: Wendy Doniger
sources.description: A scholarly analysis of Hindu mythology, providing insights into the symbolism and significance of Holi and its various interpretations.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: Symbolizes the burning away of evil and negativity.

associations.name: Spring
associations.significance: Represents new beginnings, renewal, and joy.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Saturnalia
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A festival celebrated with revelry, role reversals, and a general atmosphere of joy and freedom.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Carnival
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A festive season before Lent characterized by parades, costumes, and public celebrations, often involving a temporary suspension of social norms.

companions.name: Prahlada
companions.description: A devotee of Vishnu whose unwavering faith allowed him to survive Holika's fire, symbolizing the power of devotion and the triumph of good.

companions.name: Holika
companions.description: The sister of Hiranyakashipu who attempted to burn Prahlada alive, representing evil and the forces of negativity.

companions.name: Krishna
companions.description: Associated with the playful aspect of Holi through the legend of him coloring Radha's face.

worshipPractices.name: Holika Dahan Puja
worshipPractices.description: A ritual performed before the bonfire is lit, involving prayers and offerings to seek blessings and ward off evil.

worshipPractices.name: Radha Krishna Puja
worshipPractices.description: Worship of Radha and Krishna, particularly in regions where the Krishna legend is prominent, seeking their blessings for love and happiness.

festivals.name: Lathmar Holi
festivals.timing: Days before the main Holi celebration
festivals.description: A unique Holi celebration in Barsana and Nandgaon, where women playfully beat men with sticks (lathis).

cultural.modernLegacy: Holi continues to be a vibrant and widely celebrated festival, both in India and around the world, promoting unity, joy, and cultural exchange. It is often used as a theme for parties, events, and artistic expressions.
